title_en: As-Suyuti: The Prolific Master of Islamic Sciences
title_ar: السيوطي: الإمام الموسوعي المكثر في العلوم الإسلامية
content_en:
Jalal ad-Din Abd ar-Rahman ibn Abi Bakr as-Suyuti (849–911 AH / 1445–1505 CE) was one of the most prolific scholars in Islamic history, credited with over 500 works covering virtually every discipline of Islamic scholarship. Born in Cairo into a scholarly family of Persian origin, he received his scholarly formation under leading Egyptian and Levantine scholars of the ninth Islamic century and mastered hadith, Quranic sciences, jurisprudence, Arabic grammar, history, and biography with exceptional speed and retention.

As-Suyuti identified himself as a mujtahid — an independent legal scholar — and claimed to have reached the level of hafiz in hadith, a rank indicating memorization and mastery of at least one hundred thousand hadiths with their chains. These claims were not universally accepted by contemporaries, some of whom found his prolific output more impressive in quantity than in depth, but the best of his works demonstrate genuine scholarly accomplishment.

His formation in Quranic sciences was particularly thorough. He studied the discipline under multiple teachers and inherited a tradition of Quranic scholarship that extended back through al-Bulqini, al-Kamal ibn al-Humam, and other major Egyptian scholars. His two major works on Quranic sciences — Al-Itqan fi Ulum al-Quran and Al-Burhan fi Ulum al-Quran (the latter actually by az-Zarkashi, which as-Suyuti supplemented with his Mutawakkili) — represent the most comprehensive Arabic reference works on the Quranic sciences in the classical tradition.

As-Suyuti's scholarly career was also marked by controversy. He engaged in public disputes with contemporaries, withdrew from court patronage on principle, and spent his later years in relative isolation. His self-assessment as a renewer (mujaddid) of the tenth Islamic century was not universally shared, but his scholarship — particularly in hadith, tafsir, and the Arabic language sciences — was recognized even by critics as genuinely impressive.

His death in Cairo in 911 AH came at the beginning of the Ottoman period in Egypt, which had conquered the country just six years earlier. He left behind a legacy of extraordinary scholarly productivity that has served generations of students and scholars as a reference library in itself.
